summary: Martin warns that a major software-caused disaster killing thousands is inevitable given how pervasive code has become in our lives. When this happens, governments will investigate and impose regulations on the software industry. The nature of those regulations depends entirely on what investigators find: a self-disciplined profession with standards and practices, or chaotic hacks working under impossible conditions. He argues the industry must establish professional disciplines and self-regulation before the disaster strikes, or face draconian government control over everything from languages to working hours.
key_points: Software is now everywhere—from pacemakers to traffic signals—and we constantly put our lives in the hands of code written under pressure
A catastrophic software failure killing thousands is statistically inevitable; it's only a matter of when
Government response to such a disaster will be determined by what they find: professional discipline or chaos
The industry currently lacks defined professional standards, practices, and disciplines
Self-regulation now is the only way to prevent oppressive external regulation later
money_quotes: How many times per day do you put your life in the hands of an 'if' statement written by some twenty-two year old at three in the morning, while strung out on vodka and redbull?
The probability is not high that it will happen today, or even this year; but the probability is also not zero. It will happen.
Will they find a software industry that has defined a set of professional disciplines that it requires of its members?
Mr. President, it is my sad duty to inform this august body that these developers have no record that they test their code…
Will they discover that we are all really just a bunch of undisciplined hacks?
We need to get ahead of this one before it happens. Otherwise we'll work in a government regulated profession.
key_insight: The software industry must establish and enforce professional disciplines and self-regulation before an inevitable catastrophic failure forces governments to impose external regulation on us.
